Fern Polrrrcal PRACTTcEs C orraurssroN
428 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramenro, CA 95914_2329
(9t6) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
September 22,2006
Eric Poulsen
P.O.Box2491
Olympic Valley, California 96146
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-06-158
Dear Mr. Poulsen:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r ifrir f"?..i. U,.LA on the
facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the ..Commissio.r;j ao". rrot
act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1g7s) I Fppi ops. 71.)
Do the Act's connict-or-,-"r";:,:::;rohibit you from participatin gin a
govemmental decision involving The Resort at Squaw Valley, which i"6., i.op"rty
from a company owned by a trust in which you have an benedcial ownershif interest?
CONCLUSION
Yes. By virtue of your ownership interest in the trust and the trust investments,
you have an economic interest in The Resort at Squaw Valley as a source of income to
you and are prohibited under the Act from particiiating in any govemmental decision
involving that economic interest.
You are an elected director of the Squaw Valley Public Service District. The
Squaw valley Public service District (the "bistrict,) is a county wut", Dirt i;.
You are the trustee and a beneficiary of the poulsen Long Term Trust (the
"Trust"). The Trust has an ownership interest in the Poulsen tand Company f.pLC,,; as
' Govetnm"nt Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
1 8 I 09 - 1 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
FACTS


File No. 4-06-158
Page No. 2
a limited partner. The Trust owns the majoity share in PLC. The general partner is a
separate entity called Squaw Valley Associates, Inc.
PLC owns property on which there is a long-term lease with The Resort at Squaw
creek (the "Resort')' This property is used by the Resort for a portion oilts gorrcourse.
The Resort pays a lease fee to pLC for the property.
The Resort is currently in negotiation to obtain a "will serve,, permit from the
District to receive water, sewer, and fire permits for their phase 2. A;a director, you
have chosen not to patticipate in decisionmaking on this issue due to anypossibilities of a
conflict of interest. However, the District believes thatitwould be beneficial to have the
patticipation of the whole district board in reviewing, discussing, considerirrt, *a
rendering a decision in this matter.
In a subsequent telephone conversation on August 28,2006,you stated that the
Trust owns 98 percent of PLC with the genelll partner, Squaw Valley Associates, Inc.,
owning the remaining two-percent interest. You own a oni-eight beneficial interest in 94
percent of the Trust, equaling an 11.75 percent interest in the Trust. The Resort pays a
yeatly fee of $80,000 on the property itleases from PLC. You have also indicated that
you are a beneficiary of the Trust and you receive income from the Trust.
ANALYSIS
PoruNtran CoNrr,rcr or INTEREST
The Act's conflict_-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused uy trreir own finan"ial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them.,, (Section g1001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official fronr-making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influenc"i'gorr"*-ental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commissionaas adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualitring conflict of interest. (Regulation t AzOolt; ) Th; general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public ofnciat makes a
govelrimental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
Srrps 1 & 2: Anr You A Punr,rc orrrcr,tr, Mar<rNG, PARTICIPATING IN MAKTNG, oR
INrr,urNcrNG A GovnmwrENTAL DECrsroN?
As an elected director of the Squaw Valley Public Service District, you are a public
official under the Act. (Section 8204s.) Consequently, you may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use your official position to innuenc e anydecisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests. As a
member of the District board, you will be called uporrto consider whether the District


File No. 4-06-158
Page No. 3
should approve or d-isapprove an application from the Resort to receive water,sewer, and
fire permits- Therefore, you will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using
your official position to influence a govemmental dicision.
Srnp 3: Do You Hayr a PorrcNrr,Ir,r,vDrsgUALIrYINGECoNoMIC INTEREsT?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateial
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five "rr.r-"rut"d economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a);regulation lg703.l(a)); or in which
he or she is a directoq officer, partner, trustee, "*ploy"", or holds anyposition of
management (section 87 1 03 (d); regulation I g7 03 .1 (b));
o An economic inJerest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section g7103(b); regulation 1g703.2);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to thellcision (section g 7I03(c);
regulation 18703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section S7103(e); r"giiutiJ"
18703.4);
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects,, rule
(section 87 103 ; regulation 1 8703.5).
Additionally, regulati on I 823 4 (copy enclosed) provides :
"a) An official has an economic interest in the pro ratashare of the
interests in real property, sources of income and investrnents of a trust in
which the official has a direct, indirect or beneficial interest of 10 percent
or greater.
(Ul For purposes of this section, the interests of the official include
those of the official, spouse and dependent children regarding interests in
real property and investments and those of the official-and spouse
regarding sources of income.
(c) Forpurposes of determining whether an official has an
economic interest in interests in real property, sources of income, and


File No. 4-06-158
Page No. 4
investments of a trust, the official has a direct, indirect or beneficial
interest in a trust if the official is:
(1) {...}
(2) A beneficiary and:
(A) presently receives income; or
(B) Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal.
For purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocable future
right to receive income or principal if the trust is irrevocable, unless one of
the following applies:
(i) Powers exist to consume, invade or appoint the principal for the
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official and- such powers aL not
limited by an ascertainable standard relating to the healih, education,
support or maintenance of the beneficiaries; or
(ii) Under the terms of the trust, someone other than the official
can designate the persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust p.op".ty o,
income.
(d) For the purposes of this section, an official does not have a
direct, indirect, or beneficial interest in a trust solely because the official is
a trustee or co-trustee. However, income received for the performance of
trustee seryices is income as defined in Govemment Code Section g2O3O.
Economic.rnJerests rdentified: Applying the above rules, you have the
following economic interests:
First: You have an economic interest in the Trust as a business entity because you
are a trustee of the Trust, (regulation 1s703.1(b)) and because you own an interest in the
Trust worth $2,000 or more. (Section 97103(a) and regulation i szo:. t 1a;.;
Second: You indicate that you have a beneficial ownership interest in the Trust
equal to approximately 1 1.75 percent. PLC is one of the investments of the Trust, as the
Trust owns a 98-percent interest in PLC. Because you meet the requirements under
regulation 18%a@)Q) as a beneficiary and in that you receive income and have an
irrevocable right to firture income, you have a economic interest in the pro rata share of
the investments of the Trust, including PLC. (Section82034and regulation 1g234(a).)
Accordingly, because your pro rata share of the investment in pLC [ *ortt $i,ooo o,.
more (regulation 1 8703. I (a)), you have an economic interest in pLC, as an investment of
the Trust.
Third: PLC receives income from the Resort in the form of lease payments on
the property owned by PLC. The amount the Resort pays to pLC for the leased property
is equal to $80,000 per year. Section 82030 further piovides that income of an individual


File No. 4-06-158
Page No. 5
includes "aproratashareofanyincome of anybusiness entityortrustinwhichthe
individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or benefici ally, a I0-percent interest or
greater' Because Y!! have 71.75 percent interest in the Trust, and the Trust owns 9g-
percent of PLC, and PLC receives $80,000 per yearin income on its lease property from
the Resort, the Resort is a source of income to you in an amount equal to o^r greater than
$500 over the L2-monthperiod prior to the dec-ision. Therefo.", yo., have an"economic
interest in the Resort as a source of income to you. (Regulation 1g234.)
You have not provided any facts relatin gto any other economic interests you may
bave. -Accordingly, our conflict of interest *ulyrir is iimlted solely to the utor"
identifi ed economic interests.
srnp 4: Anr Youn Ecoxovrrc rNrrnnsrs Drrucrr,y oR TNDTRECTLyTNvoLvED rN
TIIE GOYERNMENTAL DNCTSTON?
"In order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic.interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
govemmental decision.', (Regulation I g 704(a).)
Regulation I 87 04.1 (a) states:
"a) A^person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, app"il,or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the official's ug"r"f A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves tf," iss.rance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,,
Regulation 187 04.1 (b) then provides:
"(b) If a business entity, source of income, or source of a gift is
directly involved in a governmental decisior, uppiy the materialiiy
standards in california code of Regulations, riilei, section 1g705.1(b),
section 18705.3(a), or section ls705.a@), respectively. If a business
entity, source of income, or source of a gift is not directly involved in a
governmental decision, aryly the materiality standards in California Code
of Regulations, Title 2, section 1g705.1(c), section 1g705.3(b), or section
I 87 0 5 .4(b), respectively.,,


File No. A-06-158
Page No. 6
You have an economic interest in the Resort as a source of income to you as
explained above, and that economic interest is directly involved in the decision as an
applicant or named party and the subject of the pror""ding under regulation
18704.1(a)(1) ardQ\
Neither PLC nor the Trust are applicants or named parties or the subject of the
proceeding under regulation 1870aJ@)Q) or Q). Therefore, these economic interests are
indirectly involved in the decision.
Srnp 5: Marrnr,qlrry Sr.tNnano
Regulation 18705.3(a) sets forth the materiality standard pertinent to an economic
interest that is a source of income and. directly rnvolvidin the decision:
"(a) Directly invorved sources of income. Any reasonabry
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a
public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the
official's agency, is deemed mateial.,,
Because theResort is directly involved in the governmental decision, any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on it is deemeJmaterial.
Regulation 1s705.1(c) sets forth the materiality standard pertinent to an economic
interest that is u b".t,|^e,st.,:ltiry,urd,ls, indirectly rnvolvedin the decision. For relatively
srnall business entities2, the materiality standard set forth in regulatio n 1g705.1(c)(a)
applies:
"(c) Indirectly invorved business entities. The folrowing
mateiality standards appty when a business entity in which apublic
official has an economic interest is indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. If more than one of the following subdivisions is applicable to
the-business entity in question , apply the subdivision with the 
^highest
dollar thresholds.
fl\.../
(4) If the business entity is not covered by
the financial effect of a governmental decision on
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
subdivisions (c)(1)-(3),
the business entity is
(A) The governmental decision will resurt in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
' Because you have not provided any informationregarding the size of the business entities, (pLC
Td ry lryst) for purposes of example only, we are assuming-that tf,e business falls under the criteria
identified herein' You should "ta*ir" the entire regulation Jit d to determine which materially standard
applies, based on the size ofthe business. 
.


File No. 4-06-158
Page No. 7
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year inthe amount of $s,ooo o, -o."io.,
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000
or more."
If the financial effect of the decision on PLC or the Trust meets any of the
thresholds identified above, the financial effect is considered material. However, because
the financial effects of the govemmental decision on the Resort are alreadydeemed to be
material, you have a conflict of interest in participating in the decision due to your
economic interest in the Resort, irrespective of the ^it"iulfinancial effects of the
decision on PLC or the Trust.
Srnp 6: Rrasouarr,y FonrsrEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).j Whether the
financial consequences of a govemmental decisiin are substantiaily likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (t975) 1 FppC Ops. 19g.)
Srrps 7 aNn 8: Pusl,rc GrNnnar,r,y& LTcaT,LYREQUIRED PARTICIPATIoN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the ,,public generally,, or
"legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable herein. Ac"cordingly, we
have not provided an analysis involving those steps.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
By:
Lenkeit
S enior Counsel, Legal Division

financial consequences of a govemmental decisiin are substantiaily likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (t975) 1 FppC Ops. 19g.)
